Let's examine an award-winning study that my students and I published using guests at a variety of hotels. My team and I wanted to see if we could persuade people to reuse their towels more often through the use of the principle of reciprocation. We put one of three different cards in hotel rooms. All of the cards asked guests to reuse their towels, but the cards each differed in a small but important way. The first card had an environmental appeal, like the type you've seen in hotel rooms before. Its message said, help save the environment. A second card requested that hotel guests partner with the hotel to save the environment and offered an interesting reward for compliance. If guests reuse their towels, the hotel would then donate some of the savings to an environmental cause. A final card used a reciprocation appeal. We are doing our part to save the environment. Can we count on you? Followed by crucial information that the hotel had already given a contribution to an environmental cause in the name of their guests. With the cards in place, my team and I worked with the hotels to keep track of how many towels were reused in the rooms. Here's what we found. The standard environmental appeal of help save the environment did okay. It got 35.1% of guests to reuse their towels. Do you think the reward condition did better? Actually, that reward-oriented message did worse than the simple environmental appeal. Only 30.7% of people encountering that card reuse their towels. We suspected that that was because people didn't like being bribed by such a small reward to reuse their towels. So which appeal did best? The reciprocity condition that stated that the hotel had already contributed to an environmental cause in their guests' name did the best. It persuaded 45.2% of people to reuse their towels. Why do you think this appeal worked best and by such a wide margin? Because gifts create an obligation to repay. Hotel guests in the reciprocity condition felt that they should reciprocate the gift they had already received from the hotel, even though it was a small one. So although a small reward didn't work at all, a small gift worked very well, producing a big, big effect.
